sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the current state more carefully. Looking at the segmentation:
- id=6: N (green) at cols 27-29, rows 30-32 - this is the green frame with white center (hash=df604ed27e7e83b0)
- id=9: W (white) at [31, 28] - the white center pixel
- id=8: N (green) at cols 42-44, rows 30-32 - this is the green frame with black center (same hash=df604ed27e7e83b0)
- id=7: w (white) at cols 30-32, rows 30-32 - this is the white square that was between the green frames
Wait, both green frames have the same hash (df604ed27e7e83b0). This means they're the same "type" of object. But one has a white center and one has a black center.
Looking at the image, I can see:
- Left panel: dark frame at bottom-left
- Middle: green frame with white center near the purple bar
- Right panel: dark frame at top-right, green frame with black center near it
The green frame with white center (id=6) is at cols 27-29 now (after 4 RIGHTs from original 18-20). Each RIGHT moves it 3 columns right, so 4 × 3 = 12, from 18 to 30... but it's at 27-29. Let me recalculate.
Original: cols 18-20 (center at col 19)
After 1 RIGHT: cols 21-23 (center at col 22) - but wait, the first LEFT moved it to 15-17, then RIGHT moved it back to 18-20.
Actually, let me reconsider. The initial state had the green frame at cols 18-20. After LEFT, it went to 15-17. After RIGHT, it went back to 18-20. Then after 4 more RIGHTs, it should be at 18+4*3 = 30, so cols 30-32. But the segmentation shows it at cols 27-29.
Hmm, maybe the movement isn't exactly 3 columns per step. Or maybe the segmentation is grouping things differently now.
Let me look at the ASCII again:
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwNNNccccwwwwwg`
- NNN at position 21 (0-indexed from the substring start at col 8), so actual col = 8+21 = 29... wait let me count more carefully.
Actually, the substring starts at index 8 of the line. Let me count:
`gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwNNNccccwwwwwg`
Position 0: g
Position 1-19: w (19 w's)
Position 20-22: NNN
Position 23-25: www
Position 26-30: ppppp
Position 31-33: www
Position 34-36: NNN
Position 37-40: cccc
...
So NNN starts at position 20 in the substring, which is col 8+20 = 28. So the green frame is at cols 28-30.
And the second NNN starts at position 34, which is col 8+34 = 42. So the other green frame is at cols 42-44.
OK so the green frame with white center moved from cols 18-20 to cols 28-30 (10 columns right over 4 moves = 2.5 columns per move? That doesn't make sense).
Wait, I need to be more careful. Let me recount the original position.
Original row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwg`
Position 0: g
Position 1-10: w (10 w's)
Position 11-13: NNN
So the first NNN starts at position 11, which is col 8+11 = 19. So the green frame was at cols 19-21.
Hmm, but the segmentation said boundary was [[30, 18], [30, 20], [32, 20], [32, 18]], which means rows 30-32, cols 18-20. That's a 3x3 area.
Let me just focus on what I can observe: the green frame with white center moves when I press arrow keys. It seems to move in steps of about 3 columns. The goal might be to position it somewhere specific.
Looking at the image, the green frame with black center is now on the right panel, near the dark frame. Maybe the goal is to move the green frame with white center to the right panel as well, or to some specific position.
Let me try to understand the game better by exploring more. Let me try moving UP from the current position to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try UP to see if the green frame with white center can move vertically
# Also let me check if there's a pattern - maybe I need to move the green object 
# to overlap with the dark frame on the right panel

# First, let me understand the current layout better
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current objects of interest:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'c', 'p') and n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} hash={n['hash']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")

# The dark frame on right panel: id=4 (c) at [[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 45], [29, 44]]
# The green frame with black center: id=8 (N) at [[30, 42], [30, 44], [32, 44], [32, 42]]
# The green frame with white center: id=6 (N) at [[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]]

# Let me try moving UP to see vertical movement
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
